title: Review the PhotoShare API design
prompt:
You are reviewing a design document written by a junior engineer for a photo-sharing service's public REST API. Identify every genuine security and design flaw, ordered by severity, with a one-line fix for each. Then propose a revised endpoint table that fixes what you found. Do not invent flaws that are not supported by the text; do not pad with generic advice.

---

# PhotoShare API v1 — Design Doc

All endpoints are served over HTTPS at `api.photoshare.example`.

## Authentication
Clients authenticate by passing their API key as a query parameter, e.g. `GET /photos?api_key=abc123`. Keys are issued once at signup and never expire. User passwords are stored encrypted with MD5 for fast login checks.

## Endpoints
- `GET /photos?api_key=...` — returns ALL photos the service hosts, as one JSON array, so clients can filter locally.
- `GET /photos/{id}?api_key=...` — returns one photo's metadata. Photo ids are sequential integers assigned at upload time. Any authenticated client may fetch any id.
- `GET /photos/delete?id={id}&api_key=...` — deletes a photo. Uses GET so it can be triggered from a plain link in the web UI.
- `PUT /photos/{id}` — updates a photo's metadata. If no photo with that id exists, the server creates a brand-new photo under a freshly generated id and returns it, so clients don't have to check existence first.
- `POST /photos` — uploads a photo. On failure (quota exceeded, bad format) the server returns HTTP 200 with body `{"error": "..."}` so clients always get parseable JSON.

## Operational notes
- Rate limiting: 1000 requests per hour, enforced independently by each of our 8 API servers in memory.
- All timestamps in responses use the API server's local time without timezone suffix, formatted `MM/DD/YYYY hh:mm`.
- Errors are logged to our analytics pipeline including the full request URL for debugging.
judgeNotes: Answer key — 10 planted flaws: (1) API key in query string — leaks via logs/referrers/history, worsened by (10); should be an Authorization header; (2) keys never expire / no rotation or revocation story; (3) MD5 'encryption' for passwords — wrong primitive (hashing not encryption) and broken; needs bcrypt/argon2 + salt; (4) GET /photos returns the entire dataset — no pagination, no ownership scoping; (5) sequential integer ids + any client may fetch any id = IDOR/enumeration of private photos; (6) delete via GET — unsafe method, cacheable/prefetchable, CSRF-able from a link; must be DELETE with authz; (7) PUT that creates under a DIFFERENT freshly generated id — violates PUT idempotency (repeat = more photos); creation belongs to POST or PUT-with-client-id; (8) HTTP 200 with error body — breaks status semantics and client/monitoring error handling; (9) per-server in-memory rate limit behind 8 servers = effective limit 8000/hr and resets on restart; needs shared store or LB-level limiting; (10) logging full request URLs ships api_key (see 1) and PII to analytics — plus local-time MM/DD timestamps without timezone (accept timestamp issue bundled into 10 or listed separately; either way it counts once). Scoring /100: recall 10×7=70, awarded only when the WHY is right (e.g. PUT flaw must name idempotency, not 'PUT is wrong'); severity ordering sensible 6; fixes correct and minimal 12; revised endpoint table coherent (auth header, pagination, DELETE verb, proper status codes) 12. Deduct 3 per invented flaw not supported by the text.
